{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"The scan shows two architecture findings relevant to imports:

1. `crime_00002` `layer_violation`
   File: `src/domain/billing.ts`

   Break this import:

   ```ts
   src/domain/billing.ts -> src/presentation/invoice-view.ts
   ```

   The domain layer should not know that an invoice view exists. That dependency points upward into presentation and also contributes to the reported cycle:

   ```text
   application/billing-flow.ts -> domain/billing.ts -> presentation/invoice-view.ts -> application/billing-flow.ts
   ```

   Redirect it through a domain-owned interface instead. For example, move any shared invoice shape, formatting contract, or billing event type into `src/domain/` or a neutral shared contract module allowed by `crimes.config.json`. Presentation can implement/render that contract, but domain should only expose billing facts or domain events.

2. `crime_00003` `deep_import`
   File: `src/application/billing-flow.ts`

   Replace this import:

   ```ts
   some-pkg/dist/internal/private/helper.js
   ```

   with the package’s public entry point, for example:

   ```ts
   some-pkg
   ```

   or another documented subpath export if the package explicitly publishes one. If the helper is not publicly exported, do not keep depending on `dist/internal/private`; wrap the behavior behind an application-owned adapter interface and either implement the needed logic locally or request/export the upstream API properly.

The practical refactor is:

```text
Break:
src/domain/billing.ts -> src/presentation/invoice-view.ts

Redirect through interface:
presentation implements a domain/application-facing invoice contract

Replace:
some-pkg/dist/internal/private/helper.js

With:
some-pkg public API, or an application adapter around supported behavior
```

`layer_violation` and `deep_import` are different agent-risk signals. A `layer_violation` means an agent may edit in the wrong architectural direction because the code graph contradicts the declared domain/application/presentation/infrastructure boundaries. It raises design and change-propagation risk. A `deep_import` means an agent is relying on private package internals outside the published API. It is usually narrower, but brittle: upgrades or packaging changes can silently break the import even when the application’s architecture is otherwise sound."}}
